text: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. This classic pangram contains every letter of the English alphabet at least once.
Machine learning models learn patterns from data. They generalize these patterns to make predictions on unseen examples.
Neural networks consist of layers of neurons. Each neuron applies a weighted sum followed by a nonlinear activation function.
Gradient descent optimizes model parameters by iteratively moving in the direction that reduces the loss function value.
Transformers use self-attention to weigh the importance of different parts of the input when producing each output element.
Convolutional neural networks are particularly effective for image recognition tasks due to their ability to capture spatial hierarchies.
Recurrent neural networks process sequential data by maintaining a hidden state that carries information across time steps.
Batch normalization stabilizes training by normalizing layer inputs, allowing higher learning rates and reducing sensitivity to initialization.
The softmax function converts logits into a probability distribution, commonly used as the final layer in classification networks.
Cross-validation splits data into multiple folds to evaluate model performance more robustly than a single train-test split.
Reinforcement learning agents learn optimal behavior through trial and error, receiving rewards or penalties for their actions.
Natural language processing enables computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language in useful ways.
Feature engineering transforms raw data into informative features that improve the performance of machine learning algorithms.
Ensemble methods combine predictions from multiple models to produce more accurate and robust results than any single model.
Hyperparameter tuning searches for the optimal configuration of parameters that control the learning process itself.
Dimensionality reduction techniques like PCA compress high-dimensional data into fewer dimensions while preserving important structure.
Generative adversarial networks consist of a generator and discriminator that compete, producing increasingly realistic synthetic data.
Attention mechanisms allow models to focus on relevant parts of the input, dramatically improving performance on long sequences.
Pre-training on large corpora followed by fine-tuning on specific tasks has become the dominant paradigm in modern NLP.
Distributed training splits computation across multiple GPUs or machines, enabling training of models that exceed single-device memory.
